article_title: My entire productivity stack is just these 4 Docker containers
core_thesis: The author argues that self-hosting a minimal set of productivity tools via Docker containers reduces app clutter, centralizes data control, and enhances privacy and workflow efficiency. By moving away from scattered cloud services, they achieve a streamlined, self-managed productivity stack tailored to their needs.
detailed_summary: The article describes the author's journey from juggling multiple cloud-based productivity apps to self-hosting a core set of four Docker containers. The motivation was to reduce account switching, regain control over data, and avoid reliance on third-party servers. The four containers are: Super Productivity (task management with time tracking), PhotoPrism (AI-powered media organization), AFFiNE (block-based note-taking and project management with an Edgeless Canvas), and Excalidraw (infinite whiteboard for sketches and diagrams). Each tool is self-hosted to ensure privacy, offline access, and long-term data ownership. The author emphasizes that this setup has made them more intentional about app usage and simplified their digital life.

entities.name: Nolen Jonker
entities.role: Author of the article and user of the described productivity stack.

entities.name: Super Productivity
entities.role: Open-source task manager with time tracking, notes, and alarms.

entities.name: PhotoPrism
entities.role: AI-powered media organizer for images and videos.

entities.name: AFFiNE
entities.role: Block-based note-taking and project management tool with Edgeless Canvas.

entities.name: Excalidraw

entities.name: Docker
entities.role: Containerization platform used to self-host the productivity tools.

key_data_points: Super Productivity stores all data locally by default; self-hosting ensures full privacy and independence from third-party sync services like Dropbox. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: local data control reduces attack surface and prevents cloud-based data leaks.)

PhotoPrism uses AI to automatically tag and classify media by faces, locations, dates, and objects, but self-hosting keeps files off third-party AI servers. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: demonstrates how to run AI workloads locally for privacy.)

AFFiNE can be connected to external AI providers even when self-hosted, retaining AI retrieval and interaction capabilities. (🟢 Relevant to your programming: shows how to integrate AI APIs with self-hosted apps.)

Excalidraw is primarily collaborative but can be used solo; self-hosting allows offline access and full control over diagram storage. (🟢 Relevant to your productivity: provides a self-hosted alternative to cloud whiteboards.)

The author reduced their productivity stack to just four Docker containers, which run in the same environment on their own machine. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: demonstrates a minimal, maintainable self-hosted setup.)
methodology_steps: 1. Identify productivity needs: task management, media organization, note-taking, and whiteboarding.
2. Choose self-hostable open-source tools: Super Productivity, PhotoPrism, AFFiNE, and Excalidraw.
3. Deploy each tool as a Docker container on a local server or machine, ensuring they run in the same environment.
4. Configure each container for local data storage and optional external integrations (e.g., AI providers for AFFiNE).
5. Access the tools via web interface or desktop app, and manage them through Docker commands or a UI like Portainer.
conclusions: Self-hosting a minimal productivity stack via Docker reduces app clutter and centralizes data control, leading to a more intentional and efficient workflow. The author found that four containers cover their core needs without the overhead of multiple cloud services.

Privacy and data ownership are key benefits: self-hosting eliminates reliance on third-party servers and keeps sensitive files (e.g., media, notes) under the user's control, which is especially important for those concerned about AI training or data breaches.

The approach is scalable and adaptable: users can spin up or replace containers as needed, making it suitable for both personal and professional use. The author's experience shows that a small set of well-chosen tools can replace a larger, more fragmented stack.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in homelabbing and DevOps, as it provides a concrete example of self-hosting productivity tools using Docker. You can replicate this setup to gain hands-on experience with containerization, local AI integration, and data privacy. For your Red Teaming practice, understanding how self-hosted apps store and sync data locally can inform attack vectors or defense strategies. As a student, the productivity tools themselves (especially Super Productivity for time tracking and AFFiNE for note-taking) can help you manage projects and study materials more effectively. The article also encourages a minimalist approach to tool selection, which can reduce digital clutter and improve focus.
extra_advice: Consider extending this stack by adding a reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx Proxy Manager) and a VPN (e.g., WireGuard) to securely access your self-hosted tools remotely. This will give you practical experience with network security and remote access, valuable for both homelabbing and Red Teaming.
